I know you guys aren't on board with
shrinking the balance sheet, but let's

problem here, and you guys are still
expanding the balance sheet. Can we at
least meet halfway and stop this
program?" I think that's what the
conversation's going to be. It should
be.
It should be. The Fed is monetizing US
government debt with inflation on

annualized basis. They should probably
stop monetizing US government debt and
inflating a bubble in the equity market.
Now,

know, but they should probably stop if

economic oxygen at the bottom of the
cave that are suffering from the
nationwide affordability crisis that
Powell and his colleagues can severely
contributed to.
The marginal reduction in crowding out
by the US Treasury is having a positive
impact on the repo market with the
three-month three-week moving average of
the SOFR IRBs spread at the lowest level
since late Q3. The key risk to asset
markets is the Fed extrapolating this
dynamic and taking a victory lap on
reserve management purchases, which they
clearly could do here based on the
signal.
It's something they may do anyway in
terms of shutting off the program amid
the dramatic acceleration in inflation
across all key metrics. Again, I just
went through every key metric of
inflation, and it's either in a strong
positive impulse at a level that's well
above trend and the Fed's 2% target
and/or it's in a weak positive impulse
at a level that's well above trend and
the Fed's 2% target. So, again,
you got to assume that reserve
management purchases are going to get
shut off in the coming months. And the
big risk is if they start actually
hiking interest rates. So, with $800
billion in AI CapEx currently projected
for 2026 and another 1.2 trillion
currently projected for 2027. And this
is currently projected because these
numbers keep going up every earning
season.

estimates of the neutral fed funds rate
and R-star and now sees the Fed's
current policy setting as ever so
slightly easy as a result. While AI is
unquestionably disinflationary in the
long term, it is acting as a massive
demand shock for resources and capital
in the interim.
Again, let me read that again. While AI
is unquestionably disinflationary in the
long term, it is acting as a massive
demand shock for resources and capital
in the interim. This is adding upward
pressure on inflation and upward
pressure on yields. Again, they're doing
drive-by debt deals in the in the tens
of of billions of dollars now and
they're now actively competing with the
Treasury and the rest of the global
fixed income market. So, that's a upward
positive shock to yields from the set of
of neutral rate and R-star. And then
obviously, there you know

they're they're they're got to build
data centers, they got to build stuff.

going to drive up the prices of
construction. It's going to drain
resources from other sectors of the
economy because they're throwing money
at solving this problem. I wrote my
senior thesis at Yale on why the US
cannot have a single-payer health care
system. And my core
thesis was, if you have a basically a
good that that can be infinitely
demanded as you know, based on the
government's you know, demand demand
from the government, which is

infinitely demanded good in a market

the other sectors of the economy and

was very right about Obamacare.

So, the key takeaway here, if you look
at this chart, so we the blue line in
this in top panel
is the OIS derived neutral rate. So, we
we we interpolate the market's estimate
of neutral
by looking at the floor fed funds rate
with the market pricing of the floor fed
funds rate in a cutting cycle with the
market pricing of the terminal fed funds
rate in a hiking cycle. And right now,
the market pricing of the floor fed
funds rate, which is the minimum value
on the IS curve out five full years, is
now above the effective fed funds rate,
which means now the Fed is now ever so
slightly in an easing bias based on this
upward movement in the neutral rate. The
market is essentially saying AI is this
massive demand shock in the economy and
it's draining resources from other
sectors of the economy and putting
upward pressure on inflation and upward
pressure on yields because it's also
draining capital from other sectors of
the economy and that's a positive shock
to the neutral rate, positive shock to
R-star here as well when we deflate the
neutral rate estimate by the five-year

interpret interpretation of AI's interim
impact on the neutral rate and R-star is
correct, then the Fed is falling behind
the curve and needs to get and needs to
hike at least once just to abandon its
current easing bias. Again, let me read
that again. If our interpretation of
AI's interim impact on the neutral rate
and R-star is correct, which we think it
is obviously, then the Fed is falling
behind the curve and needs to hike at
least once just to abandon its current
easing bias. So, when instead of looking
at the floor fed funds rate in this
chart, we're look using the terminal fed
funds rate in this in this chart here
and assuming that we're now in a hiking
cycle. We're at the very beginning of
what could potentially be a hiking
cycle. So, now if you look at the
terminal fed funds rate, that number is
36 basis points above the effective
funds rate. So, the Fed needs to hike at
least once just to kind of get back to
to neutral. Right now, it's it's falling
behind the curve in a way that is, you
know, fanning the flames of incremental
inflation pressure, fanning the flames
of nominal GDP because it's not keeping
up with the market's expectation of
where neutral and R-star are. And so,
obviously R-star is rising on a terminal
rate basis as well. That's that's how
you get you know, 85% drawdown in the
Nasdaq 100. This is what we what we saw
in the
in the dot-com bust. You know, again,
that's where I think we're headed from a

liquidity and ultimately you might have
